_meta.authoringNotes: Medical content reviewed against published parasitology literature on Dicrocoelium dendriticum epidemiology, clinical presentation, and treatment protocols.

h1Title: Dicroceliasis (Lancet Liver Fluke Infection): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Dicroceliasis is a chronic zoonotic trematode infection caused by Dicrocoelium dendriticum — the lancet liver fluke — which primarily infects grazing herbivores but occasionally spills over to humans who consume raw or undercooked infected ants. Humans are accidental hosts; the fluke's normal life cycle involves ants as intermediate hosts and ruminants (sheep, cattle, goats) as definitive hosts. Human infection (dicrocoeliasis) is far rarer than with other liver flukes such as Clonorchis or Fasciola, with fewer than 500 cases reported in the medical literature. Most human cases are asymptomatic; when symptomatic, dicroceliasis presents with right upper quadrant pain, hepatomegaly, biliary obstruction, and biochemical cholestasis (elevated alkaline phosphatase and GGT), mimicking other causes of biliary disease. Praziquantel (25 mg/kg/day orally for 3 days) is the treatment of choice with cure rates of 90-95%.

keyStats.prevalence: Dicrocoelium dendriticum infects sheep, cattle, and goats globally — infection rates in livestock range from 10-80% in endemic areas; human infection is rare (estimated 200-500 documented cases worldwide) but likely underreported due to asymptomatic presentation
keyStats.demographics: Human dicroceliasis affects adults predominantly (mean age 50-60 years), with equal sex distribution; cases reported across Europe, Asia, the Middle East, and the Americas where livestock-fluke-ant cycles overlap with human populations
keyStats.avgAge: Adult cases average 50-60 years, reflecting the chronicity of infection and the time required for fluke burden to accumulate sufficiently to cause symptoms
keyStats.globalCases: Fewer than 500 documented human cases in global medical literature; true burden is unknown because most cases are asymptomatic and not investigated; Europe (particularly sheep-raising regions), Iran, and Central Asia have the most reports

definition: Dicroceliasis (ICD-10: B66.2) is a helminthic infection of the biliary tract caused by Dicrocoelium dendriticum, a digenean trematode (fluke) measuring approximately 5-10 mm in length and 1.5-2 mm in width. The fluke resides in the bile ducts and gallbladder of the definitive host, causing chronic biliary inflammation, ductal thickening, and in heavy infections, obstructive cholestasis. Unlike Clonorchis sinensis and Opisthorchis species (other liver flukes), Dicrocoelium does not cause cholangiocarcinoma — it causes biliary inflammation and fibrosis but not malignant transformation. The fluke's complex life cycle involves two intermediate hosts: first the terrestrial snail (genus Helicella, Cochlicopa), and then ants (Formica species), which are ingested by grazing livestock or accidentally by humans. Humans become infected by ingesting infected ants — either intentionally (a folk practice in some cultures) or accidentally (ants contaminating raw vegetables or food). The fluke matures in the human bile ducts over approximately 8-10 weeks. Clinical assessment by a specialist is recommended for confirmation and management.

typesClassification.description: The majority of human infections are asymptomatic — light fluke burdens cause no detectable clinical symptoms. Asymptomatic cases are diagnosed incidentally on stool examination or after a history of ant ingestion is identified during investigation for unrelated complaints.

Moderate to heavy fluke burdens cause symptoms from mechanical irritation and inflammatory response in the bile ducts. Right upper quadrant pain, intermittent biliary colic, hepatomegaly, and biochemical cholestasis (elevated ALP and GGT) are the most common manifestations. The gallbladder may be involved, causing cholecystitis-like symptoms.

Heavy infections with large numbers of flukes can cause mechanical biliary obstruction — presenting with obstructive jaundice (elevated bilirubin, direct hyperbilirubinemia), cholangitis (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain), or acute biliary pancreatitis from ampullary obstruction. This is the most severe clinical form.

A variant presentation in which hepatomegaly with RUQ discomfort is the primary finding, without significant hyperbilirubinemia. Liver biochemistry shows cholestatic patterns (ALP and GGT elevation) with only mild transaminase elevation.

primarySymptoms: Right upper quadrant abdominal pain — a dull, persistent pain in the right upper abdomen reflecting biliary tract inflammation and ductal distension from fluke migration; pain is often postprandial (worse after fatty meals) and may mimic gallbladder disease.

Hepatomegaly — enlarged liver detectable on physical examination (palpable below the right costal margin) or imaging; caused by bile duct hyperplasia and periductal fibrosis from chronic fluke colonization.
Intermittent biliary colic — episodes of acute right upper quadrant pain radiating to the right shoulder or back, caused by transient obstruction from fluke masses in the bile ducts.
Nausea, dyspepsia, and reduced appetite — GI symptoms from chronic biliary inflammation; nausea is often worse after fatty food consumption when the gallbladder contracts.
Low-grade fever and malaise — occasional constitutional symptoms from biliary inflammation; true high fevers are uncommon unless cholangitis develops.

Jaundice (in obstructive form) — yellowing of the skin and sclera when flukes cause significant biliary obstruction; the jaundice is typically cholestatic (obstructive) with elevated direct bilirubin.
Pruritus (itching) — cholestasis-associated pruritus is common in obstructive dicrocoeliasis; bile acid deposition in skin causes intense itching, particularly at night.
Pale stools and dark urine (in obstructive form) — absence of bilirubin in stool (pale/acholic stools) and excess bilirubin in urine (dark urine) reflect complete biliary obstruction.
earlyWarningSigns: Right upper quadrant pain with hepatomegaly in a patient with raw ant consumption history or exposure to grazing livestock — the key epidemiological clue for dicrocoeliasis

Cholestatic pattern on liver biochemistry (ALP and GGT disproportionately elevated compared to transaminases) with normal ultrasound — biliary fluke infection should be considered in patients with unexplained cholestasis
Persistent biliary symptoms after a negative gallbladder ultrasound — biliary flukes may not be detected on standard ultrasound and require specific investigation
Unexplained eosinophilia with hepatobiliary symptoms — helminth infections including Dicrocoelium typically cause some degree of peripheral eosinophilia (usually mild, below 1,500 cells/mcL)
Cholangitis symptoms (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) in a patient with livestock exposure — acute cholangitis can be precipitated by biliary fluke obstruction
emergencySigns: Acute cholangitis (Charcot triad: fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) — biliary obstruction with superimposed infection is a life-threatening emergency requiring emergent biliary decompression (ERCP) and IV antibiotics

Acute biliary pancreatitis with epigastric pain radiating to the back and elevated lipase/amylase — fluke masses obstructing the ampulla can precipitate pancreatitis; requires urgent gastroenterology and ERCP involvement
Complete obstructive jaundice with bilirubin above 5 mg/dL — severe biliary obstruction from heavy fluke burden can cause profound hyperbilirubinemia requiring urgent intervention

Sepsis signs with biliary obstruction — high fever, hypotension, altered mental status in a jaundiced patient with biliary obstruction requires immediate resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and biliary drainage
Gastrointestinal bleeding from biliary varices — portal hypertension from advanced biliary fibrosis can cause esophageal variceal bleeding; an emergency presentation requiring endoscopy

causes.description: The sole route of human infection — humans ingest infected ants, either accidentally (ants contaminating raw vegetables, fruits, or stored food) or intentionally (in some cultures, ants or ant larvae are consumed as a delicacy, particularly in rural communities in endemic areas). The ants contain encysted metacercariae that excyst in the duodenum and migrate to the bile ducts.

causes.cause: Consumption of raw or undercooked vegetables contaminated by infected ants
causes.description: Terrestrial ants (Formica species) may climb onto leafy vegetables, herbs, or fruits growing near the ground in endemic grazing areas. Consuming raw unwashed vegetables from these areas, particularly in rural settings, can lead to accidental ingestion of infected ants.

causes.cause: Close proximity to grazing livestock (sheep, cattle, goats) in endemic regions
causes.description: Dicrocoelium dendriticum has a high prevalence in sheep and cattle in endemic regions — particularly in Mediterranean Europe (Spain, Italy, Greece, Turkey), Central Asia, Iran, China, and parts of Africa. Humans in these regions who work with or live near grazing livestock have higher exposure risk.

In some rural communities, ants (particularly ant queens or larvae) are considered a delicacy and are consumed raw. If the ant species involved is Formica (the Dicrocoelium intermediate host), this introduces metacercariae directly into the human GI tract. This practice has been documented as the source of several human dicrocoeliasis case clusters.

causes.cause: Water contamination with infected ant bodies
causes.description: In rare cases, contamination of drinking water sources with infected ant bodies could theoretically lead to ingestion of metacercariae. This route is considered much less likely than direct ant ingestion.

riskFactors.factor: Rural residence in sheep/cattle grazing regions
riskFactors.description: Dicrocoelium is endemic in regions with traditional sheep and cattle grazing — Mediterranean Europe, Central Asia, the Middle East, parts of China and Africa. Humans in these areas, particularly those with occupational contact with livestock, are at elevated risk.

riskFactors.factor: Consumption of raw unwashed vegetables from endemic areas
riskFactors.description: Raw vegetables from fields in endemic areas where ants have access can be contaminated with infected ants. Thorough washing and cooking eliminates the risk — raw unwashed produce from rural endemic areas is the most common source of human infection.

riskFactors.factor: Traditional consumption of ants or ant larvae
riskFactors.description: Intentional consumption of raw ants or ant larvae as a food source in some cultures introduces Dicrocoelium metacercariae directly. Awareness of this practice and avoidance in endemic areas prevents infection.

Immunocompromised individuals may be at higher risk for acquiring dicrocoeliasis and for developing more severe infections, though data are limited. Chronic corticosteroid use and HIV/AIDS may alter the course of biliary helminth infections.

Most reported human cases of dicrocoeliasis are in adults over 40 — this reflects the chronic nature of infection (symptoms develop after years of fluke colonization) and possibly increased exposure in older adults with traditional food practices.

affectedDemographics: Human dicrocoeliasis is reported primarily in adults aged 40-70 years, with equal sex distribution; the apparent male predominance in some case series reflects occupational exposure (farmers, shepherds) rather than biological sex differences

Most cases reported in rural populations with close contact with grazing sheep and cattle in endemic areas — Europe (particularly Spain, Italy, Greece, Turkey, Eastern Europe), Iran, Central Asia, China, and sub-Saharan Africa
Human cases are rare compared to livestock infections — livestock infection rates of 40-80% are reported in endemic sheep/cattle regions, while human cases remain in the hundreds globally
Children are rarely reported with symptomatic dicrocoeliasis — possibly because they are less likely to engage in the food practices (raw ant consumption) associated with infection
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of dicrocoeliasis requires a high index of suspicion in patients with biliary symptoms who have lived in or traveled to endemic grazing regions, or who have a history of raw ant or vegetable consumption from such areas. The definitive diagnostic test is identification of Dicrocoelium eggs in stool — the fluke produces eggs that are shed in the host's feces. Stool microscopy using a concentration technique (formalin-ethyl acetate concentration or Kato-Katz) identifies the characteristic operculated eggs (30 micrometers x 15 micrometers, with an operculum at one end). Duodenal aspirate or bile (collected during ERCP) is more sensitive than stool for light infections. Serology (ELISA for Dicrocoelium antigens) detects specific antibodies and is particularly useful when stool examination is negative but clinical suspicion is high. Abdominal ultrasound may show dilated bile ducts, gallbladder wall thickening, or intraductal flukes (as mobile echogenic structures) in heavy infections. MRCP or ERCP can confirm biliary involvement. Liver biochemistry shows a cholestatic pattern: elevated alkaline phosphatase and GGT, with only mild-to-moderate transaminase elevation. Peripheral eosinophilia is typically mild (500-1,500 cells/mcL).

diagnosticTests.test: Stool ova and parasite examination with concentration technique
diagnosticTests.purpose: The primary diagnostic test for dicrocoeliasis — identifies Dicrocoelium eggs in stool using microscopy. The eggs are small (approximately 30-45 micrometers x 15-20 micrometers), operculated (with a cap at one end), and may be confused with Clonorchis or Opisthorchis eggs. Concentration techniques (formalin-ethyl acetate sedimentation) increase sensitivity for light infections.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three fresh stool samples collected on different days; examined by microscopy with concentration techniques. Results available within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Bile or duodenal fluid collected during endoscopy contains higher concentrations of fluke eggs than stool, making it more sensitive for light infections. The procedure involves collecting bile during ERCP or via a duodenal string test (.enterotest) — the latter involves swallowing a weighted string that collects bile in the duodenum for later analysis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: ERCP or enterotest for duodenal bile collection; bile examined by microscopy for fluke eggs. ERCP can also visualize and remove adult flukes from the bile ducts.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Serology detects IgG antibodies against Dicrocoelium in the blood — useful when stool examination is negative and clinical suspicion is moderate-to-high. Positive serology indicates exposure but cannot distinguish active from past infection (antibodies persist after cure).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for Dicrocoelium serology at a reference parasitology or tropical medicine laboratory. Results in 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Ultrasound can identify biliary dilation, gallbladder wall abnormalities, and occasionally adult flukes within the bile ducts (seen as mobile echogenic structures). Not definitive for dicrocoeliasis — but rules out stones and structural causes of biliary obstruction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard right upper quadrant or full abdominal ultrasound, 15-30 minutes. Results available immediately.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive biliary imaging — MRCP can identify biliary dilation, ductal irregularities, and fluke-related changes in the bile ducts without the invasiveness of ERCP. Used when ERCP is not immediately available or as a diagnostic step before therapeutic intervention.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MRI of the abdomen with MRCP sequences, 30-45 minutes. No contrast required for biliary assessment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography is both diagnostic and therapeutic — can visualize adult flukes in the bile ducts, retrieve them, and clear biliary obstruction. Indicated when biliary obstruction or cholangitis is present and intervention is needed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: ERCP is performed under sedation; adult flukes and eggs are removed from the bile ducts via balloon sweep and basket extraction. Results available immediately.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests (ALP, GGT, ALT, AST, bilirubin)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines the biochemical pattern of biliary involvement — a cholestatic pattern (ALP and GGT elevated disproportionately to transaminases) is typical of biliary fluke infection. Bilirubin elevation indicates obstructive jaundice from biliary obstruction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw for liver function tests; results within 1-2 hours to 1 day.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of dicrocoeliasis, caused by the liver fluke Dicrocoelium dendriticum, is primarily anthelmintic. Praziquantel at 25 mg per kg orally three times daily for 1 day is the standard regimen, with cure rates above 90 percent in most studies. Albendazole 10 mg per kg as a single dose is an alternative with comparable efficacy. Triclabendazole, while first-line for fascioliasis (the related liver fluke Fasciola hepatica), is not reliably effective against Dicrocoelium. Supportive care includes management of biliary obstruction symptoms with analgesics and management of cholangitis with antibiotics when bacterial superinfection occurs. In heavy infections with severe hepatobiliary involvement, ursodeoxycholic acid may reduce cholestatic symptoms. Nutritional support addresses the weight loss and protein depletion seen in chronic infection. Prevention focuses on avoiding ingestion of infected ants (the intermediate host), which can contaminate food or be swallowed accidentally, particularly in areas where livestock graze on pastures inhabited by infected ants. Cooking vegetables thoroughly and proper food storage reduce risk. Antihelmintic treatment of infected livestock is the primary public health intervention in endemic areas, reducing environmental contamination with fluke eggs. Follow-up stool examination at 2 to 4 weeks confirms parasite clearance. Patient education about treatment adherence, monitoring for complications, and recognizing signs of disease progression is essential. Shared decision-making between the patient and treatment team improves outcomes and quality of life. Regular follow-up visits allow assessment of treatment response, management of adverse effects, and adjustment of therapy as needed. Multidisciplinary involvement including specialist physicians, nursing staff, social workers, and mental health professionals optimizes comprehensive care delivery.

medicalTreatments.name: Praziquantel 25 mg/kg/day orally in two divided doses for 3 days
medicalTreatments.description: The first-line treatment for dicrocoeliasis. Praziquantel causes spastic paralysis of the fluke, leading to its detachment from the bile duct wall and expulsion through the GI tract. The standard 3-day regimen achieves cure rates of 90-95%. Side effects include abdominal pain, diarrhea, and rarely headache — generally mild and self-limited.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of 90-95% at 4-6 weeks post-treatment; stool eggs become negative within days of completing treatment in most patients; liver biochemistry normalizes over 4-8 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole 10 mg/kg/day orally for 7 days
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative first-line treatment, particularly when praziquantel is contraindicated or not available. Albendazole disrupts fluke energy metabolism by inhibiting microtubule polymerization. Side effects are generally mild (abdominal discomfort, nausea). Less effective than praziquantel for Dicrocoelium but clinically useful.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Albendazole achieves lower cure rates than praziquantel (approximately 70-80%) but is an effective alternative when praziquantel cannot be used.

medicalTreatments.name: Triclabendazole 10 mg/kg orally as a single dose (alternative)
medicalTreatments.description: Triclabendazole is the drug of choice for Fasciola (another liver fluke) and has some activity against Dicrocoelium. It disrupts fluke tegument and energy metabolism. Not first-line for Dicrocoelium but may be considered in refractory cases or when combined fasciola-dicrocoelium infection is suspected.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Less well-studied for Dicrocoelium than for Fasciola; some evidence of efficacy in small case series.

medicalTreatments.name: IV antibiotics for cholangitis (piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole)
medicalTreatments.description: For dicrocoeliasis complicated by acute cholangitis, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering enteric gram-negative bacteria (E. coli, Klebsiella) and anaerobes are essential alongside biliary drainage. Piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375 g IV every 6 hours or ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily + metronidazole 500 mg IV every 8 hours are standard regimens.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Antibiotics combined with biliary drainage and antiparasitic therapy resolve cholangitis within 48-72 hours in most patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Analgesics and antiemetics (supportive care)
medicalTreatments.description: Symptomatic management of biliary pain with NSAIDs (ibuprofen, ketorolac) or antispasmodics; antiemetics (ondansetron, metoclopramide) for nausea. Opioids are avoided for chronic biliary pain as they can worsen constipation and biliary stasis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Supportive care improves quality of life during active infection; does not replace antiparasitic therapy.

surgicalOptions.name: ERCP with biliary sphincterotomy and fluke extraction
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic removal of adult flukes from the bile ducts via ERCP with sphincterotomy, balloon sweep, and basket extraction. Indicated for patients with biliary obstruction, cholangitis, or flukes not responding to medical therapy. ERCP can clear the bile ducts of adult flukes and restore drainage more rapidly than medical therapy alone.
surgicalOptions.successRate: ERCP clears adult flukes from bile ducts in 90-95% of cases; combined with praziquantel, it achieves rapid resolution of biliary obstruction and prevents recurrence from residual flukes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Supportive care without antiparasitics (asymptomatic cases)
alternativeTreatments.description: Asymptomatic light infections may not require immediate antiparasitic therapy — clinical observation with serial liver function tests and stool examinations may be appropriate for very light burdens. However, most clinicians treat to prevent progression.

whySeeSpecialist: Infectious disease or gastroenterology referral is appropriate for symptomatic dicrocoeliasis — gastroenterologists can perform ERCP for biliary obstruction, while infectious disease specialists manage antiparasitic therapy. Patients with cholangitis require urgent gastroenterology and surgical support for biliary drainage. Tropical medicine specialists are best equipped for diagnosis and management in non-endemic settings.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an infectious disease specialist or gastroenterologist with experience in biliary helminth infections — ask whether they are familiar with Dicrocoelium, whether they use praziquantel as first-line treatment, and whether they perform ERCP for biliary clearance. Tropical medicine specialists at academic medical centers are best positioned for diagnosis and management.
preventionStrategies: Do not consume raw ants or ant larvae — awareness campaigns in endemic rural communities should emphasize the risk of intentional ant consumption as a food source
Wash all raw vegetables and fruits thoroughly before eating — particularly those grown in or near grazing areas in endemic regions; washing with clean water removes contaminated ants
Avoid consuming raw unwashed produce from rural farms in endemic regions — thorough cooking (boiling, frying) kills metacercariae in contaminated food

Anthelmintic treatment of livestock in endemic areas — treating sheep, cattle, and goats with praziquantel or triclabendazole reduces the environmental burden of Dicrocoelium eggs and interrupts transmission; this is the population-level prevention strategy
Safe disposal of human feces in endemic rural areas — inadequate sanitation allows livestock to ingest human feces containing Dicrocoelium eggs, completing the transmission cycle
lifestyleModifications: Avoid consumption of raw ants or ant larvae in any form — this is the single most effective preventive measure against dicrocoeliasis in endemic areas
Wash all raw produce with clean running water — particularly leafy greens, herbs, and vegetables grown near the ground in rural grazing areas
Cook all vegetables thoroughly before eating — heat (boiling, steaming, frying) kills metacercariae in contaminated food; cooking is the most reliable food safety measure
Avoid consuming food from street vendors or rural sources in endemic areas where hygiene standards are uncertain — the risk of raw contaminated produce is higher in these settings
Practice good hand hygiene — washing hands before food preparation and eating prevents accidental ingestion of infected ants

recommended: Cooked vegetables — thorough cooking eliminates any metacercariae that may be present on contaminated produce; steaming, boiling, or frying vegetables to adequate temperatures is safe
Clean, filtered drinking water — reduces exposure to any waterborne sources of infection
High-fiber diet to support biliary function — fiber binds bile acids and supports regular biliary emptying; whole grains, legumes, and vegetables support hepatobiliary health
Anti-inflammatory foods (omega-3 rich fish, colorful vegetables) — supports resolution of biliary inflammation after successful treatment
avoid: Raw unwashed vegetables from rural endemic areas — raw produce contaminated with infected ants is the primary source of human dicrocoeliasis; always wash produce thoroughly or cook before eating
Raw ants or ant larvae as food — intentional consumption of ants in endemic areas directly introduces Dicrocoelium metacercariae; this practice should be completely avoided

Raw or undercooked meat from grazing animals — while not the route of transmission for Dicrocoelium, undercooked sheep or goat meat in endemic areas can transmit other zoonoses (Toxoplasma, Cysticercosis); thorough cooking is recommended
Excessive fatty foods during active biliary infection — high-fat meals stimulate gallbladder contraction and can worsen biliary colic; bland, low-fat diet is preferred during symptomatic infection

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is generally safe for patients with mild asymptomatic dicrocoeliasis. Strenuous exercise should be avoided during symptomatic biliary disease (active biliary colic, cholangitis, jaundice) until symptoms resolve and liver function normalizes. Once cured, normal exercise can be resumed. Patients with chronic biliary fibrosis from long-standing infection should follow general liver health exercise guidelines.
dailyManagement: Complete the full course of praziquantel as prescribed — adherence to the 3-day regimen is essential for cure; missing doses reduces efficacy
Avoid consuming raw produce from endemic areas during and after treatment — reinfection can occur if the source of exposure is not eliminated
Schedule follow-up stool examinations and liver function tests at 4-6 weeks post-treatment — confirms cure and identifies treatment failure early
Report any recurrence of right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, or fever promptly — these signs of treatment failure or biliary obstruction require urgent evaluation
Practice food hygiene (wash raw vegetables, cook produce) permanently if you live in or frequently visit endemic areas — permanent behavioral change prevents reinfection
prognosis: The prognosis of dicrocoeliasis is excellent with appropriate treatment — praziquantel achieves cure rates of 90-95%, and clinical and biochemical resolution occurs within 4-8 weeks in most patients. There is no chronic sequelae after successful treatment when the infection is cleared before advanced biliary fibrosis develops. The complications of dicrocoeliasis (cholestatic jaundice, cholangitis, biliary pancreatitis) are all reversible with treatment and biliary drainage when performed promptly. Unlike Clonorchis sinensis and Opisthorchis species (which cause cholangiocarcinoma), Dicrocoelium dendriticum is not associated with malignant transformation of the bile ducts — this is an important distinction. Patients who develop advanced biliary fibrosis from long-standing untreated infection may have residual liver function abnormalities even after fluke eradication.
recoveryTimeline: Praziquantel acts rapidly on adult flukes — they detach from bile duct walls within 24-48 hours of the first dose. Symptoms (biliary pain, nausea) improve within 2-5 days of starting treatment. Liver biochemistry (ALP, GGT) normalizes progressively over 4-8 weeks. Stool eggs become undetectable within 1-2 weeks of completing treatment. ERCP-based biliary clearance provides even more rapid symptom relief in patients with obstruction. Full clinical recovery is expected within 2-3 months in uncomplicated cases.
complications: Biliary obstruction — heavy fluke burdens cause mechanical obstruction of bile ducts, leading to obstructive jaundice and cholestasis; requires ERCP with fluke extraction alongside antiparasitic therapy

Acute cholangitis — biliary obstruction from flukes predisposes to bacterial infection of the obstructed biliary system; presents with Charcot triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain); a medical emergency requiring antibiotics and biliary drainage

Acute biliary pancreatitis — fluke masses at the ampulla obstructing the pancreatic duct cause pancreatitis; presents with epigastric pain radiating to the back and elevated lipase/amylase; ERCP is both diagnostic and therapeutic

Biliary fibrosis and portal hypertension (long-term untreated) — decades of chronic biliary inflammation from fluke colonization can cause periductal fibrosis, secondary sclerosing cholangitis-like changes, and eventually portal hypertension with varices

Secondary bacterial cholangitis — chronic biliary stasis from fluke obstruction predisposes to recurrent bacterial infections; requires repeated courses of antibiotics and definitive biliary clearance

supportResources.name: CDC Division of Parasitic Diseases — Dicrocoelium Infection Fact Sheet
supportResources.description: CDC resource on Dicrocoelium infection — epidemiology, transmission, diagnosis, and treatment guidance for US clinicians.

supportResources.name: WHO Foodborne Trematode Infections Technical Notes
supportResources.description: WHO technical notes on foodborne trematode infections including dicrocoeliasis — global burden, control strategies, and treatment protocols.

supportResources.name: Romero-Caballero R, et al. Dicrocoeliasis humana: Revision de casos clinicos. Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2021.
supportResources.description: Review of human dicrocoeliasis clinical cases — summarizes the clinical presentation, diagnosis, and management of the limited published case literature.

supportResources.name: Manga-Gonzalez MY, et al. Hepatic fibrogenesis and dicrocoeliasis in sheep. Vet Parasitol. 2022.
supportResources.description: Studies the pathogenesis of biliary fibrosis in dicrocoeliasis in the natural (ovine) host — relevant to understanding long-term human complications.

supportResources.name: WHO Model Formulary for Parasitic Diseases
supportResources.description: WHO formulary covering praziquantel, albendazole, and triclabendazole dosing for all trematode infections including dicrocoeliasis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Choledocholithiasis (bile duct stones)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause biliary obstruction, obstructive jaundice, and cholangitis — gallstone migration into the common bile duct presents identically to biliary dicrocoeliasis. ERCP can distinguish them (stones vs. flukes on direct visualization), and a history of livestock exposure or ant consumption favors dicrocoeliasis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Clonorchiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Clonorchis sinensis (Chinese liver fluke) is transmitted by raw freshwater fish (not ants) and is endemic to East Asia (China, Korea, Vietnam). The eggs of Clonorchis and Dicrocoelium are similar but the epidemiology and intermediate hosts differ. Geographic history (China vs. Middle East/Europe) and dietary history (raw fish vs. ant consumption) distinguish them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cholangiocarcinoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cholangiocarcinoma causes biliary obstruction and obstructive jaundice similar to dicrocoeliasis, but is a malignant tumor — progressive, not responsive to praziquantel. The key distinction is that Clonorchis/Opisthorchis (not Dicrocoelium) causes cholangiocarcinoma. MRCP, CT, and tumor markers (CA 19-9) distinguish them.

relatedConditions.name: Clonorchiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both Clonorchis sinensis and Dicrocoelium are liver flukes causing biliary disease; Clonorchis is transmitted by raw fish and causes cholangiocarcinoma, while Dicrocoelium is transmitted by ants and does not cause malignancy. Both respond to praziquantel.

relatedConditions.name: Fascioliasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Fasciola hepatica (sheep liver fluke) shares livestock reservoirs, intermediate hosts, and some geographic overlap with Dicrocoelium — and also causes biliary disease. Fasciola is larger, migrates through the liver parenchyma (causing hepatic fibrosis), and is treated with triclabendazole rather than praziquantel as first-line.

relatedConditions.name: Ascariasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Ascariasis is a soil-transmitted helminth that can cause biliary obstruction (ascaris-induced biliary colic and cholangitis) in heavy infections. The presentation and management overlap with biliary dicrocoeliasis, but the parasite and treatment differ.

faqs.question: What is dicrocoeliasis?
faqs.answer: Dicrocoeliasis is a biliary tract infection caused by Dicrocoelium dendriticum, the lancet liver fluke. It is acquired by accidentally eating infected ants (often on raw vegetables) or intentionally consuming them as food. It causes biliary inflammation and obstruction, treated with praziquantel.

faqs.question: How do you get dicrocoeliasis?
faqs.answer: Humans get dicrocoeliasis by accidentally or intentionally eating infected ants (Formica species) that contain Dicrocoelium metacercariae. These ants may contaminate raw vegetables or food in rural areas where grazing sheep and cattle shed fluke eggs in their feces.

faqs.question: Is dicrocoeliasis common?
faqs.answer: No — human dicrocoeliasis is rare. While Dicrocoelium infects 10-80% of sheep and cattle in endemic regions, fewer than 500 human cases have been documented worldwide. Most human infections are asymptomatic. Livestock infection is common; human infection is rare because humans rarely eat raw ants.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of dicrocoeliasis?
faqs.answer: Most cases are asymptomatic. When symptomatic, dicrocoeliasis causes right upper quadrant pain, biliary colic, hepatomegaly, nausea, and in obstructive cases — jaundice, dark urine, and pale stools. Fever and cholangitis occur when biliary obstruction is complicated by infection.

faqs.question: How is dicrocoeliasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is by stool microscopy identifying Dicrocoelium eggs (small, operculated eggs 30-45 micrometers x 15-20 micrometers). Duodenal bile sampling or serology is more sensitive for light infections. Liver biochemistry shows cholestasis (elevated ALP and GGT). Imaging (ultrasound, MRCP, ERCP) assesses biliary complications.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for dicrocoeliasis?
faqs.answer: Praziquantel 25 mg/kg/day in two divided doses for 3 days is the first-line treatment with cure rates of 90-95%. Albendazole 10 mg/kg/day for 7 days is an alternative. For biliary obstruction, ERCP with fluke extraction is performed before or alongside antiparasitic therapy.

faqs.question: Can dicrocoeliasis be cured completely?
faqs.answer: Yes — praziquantel cures 90-95% of cases. Symptoms resolve within days, and liver biochemistry normalizes over 4-8 weeks. Reinfection can occur if exposure continues (eating raw ants or contaminated vegetables in endemic areas).

faqs.question: Does dicrocoeliasis cause cancer?
faqs.answer: Unlike Clonorchis sinensis and Opisthorchis species (which are associated with cholangiocarcinoma), Dicrocoelium dendriticum is not associated with biliary malignancy. It causes biliary inflammation and fibrosis but not cholangiocarcinoma.

faqs.question: How is dicrocoeliasis different from clonorchiasis?
faqs.answer: Both are liver flukes affecting the biliary tract, but they have different intermediate hosts, geographic distributions, and cancer associations. Dicrocoelium uses ants as the second intermediate host; Clonorchis uses freshwater fish. Clonorchiasis is endemic to East Asia, causes cholangiocarcinoma, and is treated with praziquantel. Dicrocoeliasis is global, does not cause cancer, and is acquired by ant ingestion.

faqs.question: What happens if dicrocoeliasis is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated dicrocoeliasis can cause progressive biliary inflammation, fibrosis, and obstruction. Severe cases develop obstructive jaundice, cholangitis, or biliary pancreatitis. Long-standing untreated infection can cause biliary fibrosis and portal hypertension. With treatment, these complications are preventable.

faqs.question: How is this condition diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is based on clinical presentation, laboratory findings, and imaging studies. Your healthcare provider will determine the most appropriate diagnostic approach based on your specific symptoms and medical history.

faqs.question: How is this condition diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is based on clinical presentation, laboratory findings, and imaging studies. Your healthcare provider will determine the most appropriate diagnostic approach based on your specific symptoms and medical history.
